title: Between Two Castles of Mad King Ludwig

metadata.notes: Content overhaul — Wingspan teaching standard. Sources: official rulebook, Stonemaier FAQ, BGG forums, community strategy guides.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Room Tiles (147 total)
Every tile you'll draft is color-coded by room type, and each one has its own scoring condition printed right on the tile. Here's what you're working with:
- **Living rooms** (purple) — these care about the 8 tiles surrounding them (orthogonal + diagonal). Each one names a specific room type and scores 1 VP per match it finds next door, up to 8 VP.
- **Sleeping rooms** (blue) — your high-stakes tiles. They score 4 VP each if your castle has at least one of every other room type, but only 1 VP each if any type is missing.
- **Food rooms** (yellow) — these check specific adjacent positions (directly above, below, or beside, as printed on the tile) and score 2 VP for each room of a named type they find there.
- **Outdoor rooms** (green) — these scan your entire castle for rooms of a named type, scoring 1 VP per match. The catch: nothing can ever be built above an outdoor room.
- **Corridor rooms** (white) — these look at their 8 surrounding tiles for a specific wall hanging symbol, scoring 1 VP per match.
- **Utility rooms** (orange) — your big scalers. They score for connected or chained rooms of specified types with no maximum cap, making them the highest-ceiling tiles in the game.
- **Downstairs rooms** (black) — these look straight up through their vertical column, scoring 1-2 VP per room of a named type stacked above them. They must sit below the throne room row.

#### Specialty Rooms & Throne Rooms
- You'll find specialty rooms shuffled into the general supply. Each has unique scoring printed on it.
- Three specialty room types can only be earned through bonuses: **Tower** (scores for rooms below it in its column), **Fountain**, and **Grand Foyer**.
- **Each castle starts with 1 throne room tile** as its centerpiece. Your throne room shows 2 desired room types in specific positions, worth 2 VP per match. On its own, it scores nothing.

#### Bonus Cards
- The game includes 20 bonus cards (28 in the Essential Edition), drawn between rounds.
- Each card adds an end-game scoring condition to one of your castles (for example, "3 VP per set of 3 different room types").
- Once you assign a bonus card, it stays face-up. Everyone at the table can see it.

#### Other Components
- Player aids (1 per player) summarizing room types and scoring
- Score tracking pad for final scoring
- King token for determining first player and draft direction
- Rulebook with illustrated placement examples

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Core Loop
subtopics.content:
#### You're Building Two Castles at Once
Here's what makes this game click: you're simultaneously building **two castles** — one shared with the player on your left, one shared with the player on your right. At the end, your personal score equals the **LOWER** of your two castles' scores. If you pour everything into one castle and neglect the other, that weak castle drags your score down.

#### How the Game Flows
You'll play 2 rounds of tile drafting with a bonus phase sandwiched in between:

**Round 1 — Drafting Room Tiles**
1. Everyone draws a hand of **9 room tiles** from the face-down supply.
2. Look through your hand and secretly pick 2 tiles.
3. Once everyone's ready, all players reveal at the same time. Place 1 tile into your left castle and 1 into your right castle. Talk with each partner about where tiles should go.
4. Pass your remaining hand to the **left**.
5. Repeat this draft-reveal-place-pass cycle for 4 total turns. After your 4th pick, you'll have 1 tile left — discard it face-down.

**Bonus Phase (Between Rounds)**
1. Draw bonus cards (the count depends on your player count).
2. Assign 1 bonus card to your left castle and 1 to your right castle. Discard any extras.
3. Each bonus card adds an end-game scoring condition tied to that specific castle.

**Round 2 — Draft Again**
1. Everyone draws a fresh hand of 9 tiles.
2. Same drafting process, but this time you pass hands to the **right**.
3. After 4 turns, discard the last remaining tile.

#### Working With Your Partners
Every time you place a tile, you should be talking with the partner who shares that castle. You both want it to score well, but you each have a *second* castle pulling at your attention. That tension — negotiation, compromise, reading what your partner needs — is the heart of the game. A partner who feels heard will invest more in your shared castle.

#### Triggering Bonuses
When the **3rd room of any single type** lands in a castle, a bonus fires immediately — you might gain extra tiles, specialty rooms, bonus cards, or royal attendants depending on the room type. When the **5th room of a type** lands, you pick 1 specialty room (Tower, Fountain, or Grand Foyer) to place right away. Each threshold triggers only once per room type per castle, so plan your drafts to hit them.

#### Placement Rules at a Glance
- Every tile must connect orthogonally to an existing tile, building outward from the throne room.
- Your castle grows on a grid — tiles go above, below, left, or right of what's already there.
- Once placed, a tile stays put.
- **Sleeping rooms** (blue) go above the throne room row. **Downstairs rooms** (black) go below. **Nothing** goes above outdoor rooms (green).

subtopics.title: Detailed Rules
subtopics.content:
#### Setup
1. Place one throne room tile between each pair of adjacent players (in a 5-player game, that's 5 castles arranged in a circle).
2. Shuffle all room tiles face-down into a supply.
3. Shuffle bonus cards into a face-down deck.
4. Give the King token to a random starting player.
5. Hand each player a player aid.

#### Drafting Procedure
- You always draw **9 tiles** regardless of player count.
- Each turn: pick 2 tiles secretly, reveal simultaneously, place 1 in each castle, pass your remaining hand.
- Round 1 passes **left**. Round 2 passes **right**.
- After 4 turns, discard the 1 remaining tile face-down.

#### Placement Rules
- Every tile must sit orthogonally adjacent to at least one existing tile.
- Talk with your castle partner about where each tile goes — it's a shared decision.
- **Downstairs rooms** (black) must go below the throne room row.
- **Sleeping rooms** (blue) must go above the throne room row.
- **Outdoor rooms** (green) can go anywhere, but nothing may ever be placed above them.
- Once placed, tiles cannot be moved.

#### How Each Room Type Works in Practice

**Living Rooms (Purple)** — When you place a living room, look at the room type it names, then count how many of that type sit in the 8 tiles around it. That count is your VP. To get the most out of a living room, tuck it into the middle of your castle where it has the most neighbors. Maximum 8 VP per tile.

**Sleeping Rooms (Blue)** — These don't care about position at all. At game end, check whether your castle contains at least one of each of the 6 other regular room types (food, living, utility, outdoor, corridor, downstairs). If yes, every sleeping room scores **4 VP**. If you're missing even one type, every sleeping room drops to **1 VP**. Specialty rooms and the throne room do NOT count toward the 6-type requirement. Commit to collecting all types early, or skip sleeping rooms entirely.

**Food Rooms (Yellow)** — Each food room points to specific adjacent positions (above, below, or beside) and names a room type. Place the food room so those neighbors match, and you score 2 VP per match. Think of food rooms as tiles you draft *after* you know what's already sitting in your castle, so you can slot them into the right spot.

**Outdoor Rooms (Green)** — These scan your entire castle for a named room type, so position doesn't matter for scoring. What matters is the building restriction: nothing can ever go above an outdoor room. Place them at the top of columns or along castle edges where they won't block future growth.

**Corridor Rooms (White)** — Each corridor tile shows a specific wall hanging symbol. It scores 1 VP per tile bearing that symbol in its 8 surrounding spaces. When drafting corridor tiles, check which wall hangings are already clustered in your castle and nestle the corridor among them.

**Utility Rooms (Orange)** — These score for connected or chained rooms of a named type with no cap. A utility room that says "1 VP per food room in this castle" keeps growing every time you add another food room. Place utility rooms early in the game so they benefit from every tile drafted after them.

**Downstairs Rooms (Black)** — These look straight up through their column, scoring 1 or 2 VP (as printed) for each room of a named type stacked above. The taller the column, the more they score. Place them deep below the throne room, then build upward with matching tiles.

#### Bonus Phase
- Draw bonus cards between Round 1 and Round 2.
- Assign 1 card to each of your castles. Discard any extras.
- Cards stay face-up — all players can see what each castle is chasing.

#### 2-Player Variant (Ludwig)
A dummy third castle called "Ludwig" sits between the two players, creating 3 castles total. You share one castle with your opponent and one with Ludwig. The adjacent human player makes all placement decisions for the Ludwig side. Your score is still the lower of your two castles.

#### Specialty Rooms
- **Fountains** are specialty rooms, NOT outdoor rooms — they don't have the tree icon. This means you *can* build above them. Confirmed in the official FAQ.
- **Towers** score for the total number of rooms below them in their column. Place them at the top of a tall stack.
- **Grand Foyers** score as printed on their tile.

subtopics.title: Scoring & End Game
subtopics.content:
#### When the Game Ends
The game wraps up after Round 2 drafting is complete. Every game plays exactly 2 rounds plus the bonus phase — there's no variable end trigger.

#### Scoring Your Castles
Score each castle individually by adding up these categories:

1. **Room tile points** — go through every room tile and evaluate its scoring condition based on where it ended up and what's around it.
2. **Bonus card points** — check each castle's bonus card(s) against the castle's final layout.
3. **Room type set bonus** — for each complete set of all 7 room types in a castle, score bonus VP.
4. **Throne room points** — 2 VP for each matching room in the correct adjacent position.
5. **Specialty room points** — Towers, Fountains, and Grand Foyers score according to their printed conditions.
6. **Royal Attendant points** — each attendant scores 1 VP per wall hanging of its specified type anywhere in the castle.

#### Your Personal Score
- You contributed to exactly 2 castles.
- Your personal score equals the **LOWER** of your two castles' scores.
- A 70/50 split gives you 50. A 60/60 split gives you 60. Balance wins.

#### Determining the Winner
- Highest personal score wins.
- **Tiebreaker**: the player whose HIGHER castle scored more wins.
- If still tied, you share the victory.

#### What Good Scores Look Like
- First game: 40-55 VP per castle is typical
- Experienced play: 55-75 VP per castle
- Highly optimized: 75+ VP per castle

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### The Golden Rule: Balance Your Two Castles
Your score is the LOWER of your two castles. A lopsided 80/40 split gives you 40 points; a balanced 60/60 gives you 60. Never neglect either castle. Every turn, glance at both — if one is falling behind, that's where your next tile should go.

#### Which Room Types to Prioritize
- **Utility rooms (orange) scale the hardest.** A utility room scoring "1 VP per food room in this castle" grows every time a food room gets drafted. Place utility rooms early so they benefit from the entire game. No scoring cap means these can be your single highest-scoring tiles.
- **Sleeping rooms (blue) are all-or-nothing.** They score 4 VP each with all 6 other room types present, but only 1 VP without. That gap can swing 15+ points. If you're going for sleeping rooms, make sure your castle is on track for full type diversity. If it's not, skip them.
- **Outdoor rooms (green) belong on the edges.** They scan your whole castle (strong scaling), but nothing can go above them. Place them at the top of columns or along the perimeter where they won't block future building.
- **Downstairs rooms (black) want tall columns above them.** They only score for rooms in their own vertical column — not the whole castle. Place them deep below the throne room, then stack matching tiles upward.

#### Chase Those 3rd-Tile Bonuses
When the 3rd room of any type lands in a castle, a bonus fires — extra tiles, specialty rooms, bonus cards, or royal attendants. These bonuses are often worth more than any single tile's raw scoring. Reaching 3 of a type should be a deliberate goal, not something that happens by accident.

#### Talk to Your Partners
You share each castle with a different player. Tell them what you need. If your left partner is loading your shared castle with sleeping rooms, let your right partner know you need food and outdoor rooms for type diversity on the other side. The players who talk the most almost always score the highest.

#### When to Hate-Draft
Sometimes the best pick isn't the tile that helps you — it's the tile that stops an opponent from completing a set or triggering a bonus. Pay attention to what your neighbors are building, especially in the later picks of each round when hands are small.

#### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- **Neglecting one castle.** Your weaker castle IS your score. Check both every turn.
- **Ignoring adjacency.** Many rooms score based on neighbors. Dropping tiles randomly wastes points you could have had for free.
- **Hoarding one room type.** Six sleeping rooms but no food or outdoor rooms means missed set bonuses and a fragile scoring plan.
- **Burying outdoor rooms.** Nothing can go above them. If you place one in the middle of a column, you've just capped that column's growth.
- **Glossing over bonus cards.** A bonus card can be worth 10+ VP if you build around it. Assign it to the castle that already fits its condition, then keep feeding it.
- **Fighting your partner.** A slightly suboptimal tile placement that keeps your partner invested is better than a "perfect" placement that makes them give up on your shared castle.

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Welcome to Your First Game
You're sitting at the table. Between you and each neighbor sits a throne room tile — that's the heart of a castle you'll build together. You have two castles to care about: one to your left, one to your right. Grab your player aid and take a moment to look over the room type summary.

#### Round 1, Turn 1 — Your First Draft
Draw 9 room tiles from the supply. Spread them out and take your time. You're looking for 2 tiles — ideally one that works well in your left castle and one for your right. Don't overthink it yet; just pick two that have clear scoring conditions you understand. Keep them secret until everyone's ready, then all players reveal at once.

Now place 1 tile in each castle. Talk with each partner about where the tile should go. Quick reminder: downstairs rooms (black) go below the throne row, sleeping rooms (blue) go above it. Pass your remaining 7 tiles to the left.

#### Round 1, Turn 2 — Reading the Hand
You receive 7 tiles from the player on your right. This time, look at what's already in your castles. Got a food room (yellow) sitting next to an empty spot? A food room that scores for adjacency would slot in perfectly. Got a utility room (orange) scoring for a specific type? Draft more of that type to grow it. Pick 2, reveal, place, pass left.

#### Round 1, Turns 3-4 — Shrinking Options
Your hand drops to 5, then 3. Fewer choices mean you'll have to adapt. After Turn 4, discard that last tile. Now pause and take stock of both castles. Are they roughly balanced? Is either one missing a room type that sleeping rooms (blue) need for their 4 VP bonus? Make a mental note — the bonus phase is next.

#### Bonus Phase — Adding End-Game Goals
Draw your bonus cards and read them carefully. Assign the one that best fits your left castle's layout to the left, and the other to the right. These cards are now face-up for everyone to see. Your partners should build toward them too, so point out what you're chasing.

#### Round 2 — Fresh Tiles, Reversed Direction
Draw a new hand of 9 tiles. Same drafting rhythm, but this time you pass to the **right**. Your priorities now: complete room type sets if sleeping rooms are in play, build around your bonus cards, and try to hit those 3rd-tile bonuses by getting a 3rd room of any type into a castle. Keep talking with your partners — they're seeing different tiles than you, and coordination matters more in Round 2.

#### Scoring — The Moment of Truth
Score each castle tile by tile: room tile points, bonus card points, set bonuses, throne room matches, specialty room scores, and royal attendant points. Add them up. Your personal score is the LOWER of your two castles.

For a first game, 50-60 per castle is solid. If you hit 65+, you played a great game. The biggest lesson most new players learn: the castle you ignored is the one that determined your score.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Castle Balance Is Everything
Your score equals your LOWER castle. Never let one castle fall more than 10 points behind the other. If your left castle is struggling, prioritize it even if your right castle could score more with that tile. The math is unforgiving — a 75/55 split loses to a 65/65.

#### Building Room Synergy Chains
The highest scores come from rooms that amplify each other:
- **Utility rooms (orange) + a focused type:** A utility scoring "1 VP per food room" alongside 5 food rooms is already 5 VP from one tile — and it keeps growing with every food room drafted after it. Place utility rooms early to maximize their payoff.
- **Living rooms (purple) + dense clusters:** Living rooms score for specific types in their 8 surrounding tiles. Pack them into the center of your castle where they have the most neighbors, not along edges where half their scoring zone is empty.
- **Food rooms (yellow) + sleeping rooms (blue):** Food rooms push you toward specific adjacencies. Sleeping rooms push you toward type diversity. Together they naturally guide you toward a balanced, high-scoring castle.
- **Downstairs rooms (black) + tall columns:** A downstairs room scoring 2 VP for a specific type above it, with 3-4 matching rooms stacked overhead, pulls in 6-8 VP from a single tile.

#### Activating Your Throne Room Early
Each throne room scores 2 VP for matching rooms in specific adjacent positions. Identify those conditions during setup and draft for them in Round 1. A fully activated throne room can be worth 8+ VP — often the difference in a close game. Ignoring it is like leaving free points on the table.

#### Reading and Sending Draft Signals
Your drafting choices tell your partner what you're building. If you consistently place food rooms (yellow) into a castle, your partner should recognize you're building a food cluster and feed into it. Fighting your partner's signals hurts both of you — you share the castle's score. Lean into what your partner is doing, even if it wasn't your original plan.

#### Timing Your Specialty Rooms
- **Towers** score for every room below them in their column. Don't grab a Tower early — wait until you've built a column 3-4 rooms tall, then cap it with a Tower for maximum payoff.
- **Fountains** are specialty rooms, NOT outdoor rooms — they don't block building above them. Place them where an outdoor room would be too restrictive.
- **Grand Foyers** score as printed. When the 5th-room bonus triggers, evaluate each available specialty room against your castle's current state rather than defaulting to a favorite.

#### Adjusting for Player Count
- **3-4 players:** Fewer castles means each one gets more attention from both partners. Scores tend to be higher and more balanced. You can plan further ahead because fewer players are competing for the tiles you need.
- **5-7 players:** More competition in the draft. Hate-drafting matters more because key tiles disappear faster. You see fewer tiles in later picks, so your early selections carry more weight.
- **2 players (Ludwig variant):** You control one side of Ludwig completely. Invest enough in Ludwig to keep your score competitive, but don't over-invest at the expense of your shared castle with your opponent. Ludwig is a tool, not a passion project.

rulings: Fountains are specialty rooms, NOT outdoor rooms — they lack the tree icon that identifies outdoor rooms.

Sleeping rooms require at least one of each of the 6 other regular room types (food, living, utility, outdoor, corridor, downstairs) to score 4 VP. Specialty rooms and the throne room do NOT count toward this requirement.
Downstairs rooms score for the vertical column directly above the downstairs room — not the entire castle.
Nothing may ever be placed above an outdoor room tile. This is a hard placement constraint.
Each player always draws 9 tiles per round regardless of player count.
When the 3rd room of any type is placed in a castle, an immediate bonus triggers. The 5th room of a type lets players choose one specialty room (Tower, Fountain, or Grand Foyer) to place.
Bonus cards are public information — all players can see which bonuses each castle has after assignment.
In the 2-player Ludwig variant, the adjacent human player makes all placement decisions for the Ludwig side.
